blurb: An airy haze of lavender-blue over silver stems — tough, xeric and long-blooming.
spread: Woody-based clump; can self-sow & creep by rhizomes — pick a compact cultivar & deadhead to limit it
seasons: Silvery aromatic stems; lavender-blue haze midsummer–fall; silver winter structure (cut back hard in late winter)
origin: Central to Southwest Asia — Afghanistan, Pakistan, Tibet, W. Himalayas
habitat: Dry hills, steppes, and gravelly slopes in semi-arid montane zones

care.hardiness: USDA zones 5–9 — reliably hardy on the Front Range (5b–6a). It dies to the ground (or close to it) in a typical Boulder winter and regrows from the woody base each spring. The silver stems that persist through winter provide genuine structural interest and also mark where the plant lives so you don't accidentally dig it up in spring.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is non-negotiable — 6–8 hours minimum. In part shade the plant flops badly and blooms poorly. The Front Range's 300+ days of intense high-altitude sun is exactly the condition this plant evolved for in the steppes of Central Asia.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to lean, well-drained soil. It naturally prefers slightly alkaline pH (above 7.0), making Boulder's typically alkaline clay-loam an acceptable match — as long as drainage is adequate. Amend heavy clay with coarse grit or plant on a gentle slope to improve drainage. Rich, fertile, or constantly moist soil causes floppy, rank growth; skip heavy compost. Rocky or sandy soils are fine.[1][2]
care.water: Xeric once established. Water deeply once or twice a week in the first growing season to develop deep roots; thereafter, established plants are genuinely drought-tolerant and need supplemental irrigation only during prolonged heat or drought (roughly every 2–3 weeks in summer). Overwatering and poor drainage invite root rot — the main way to kill this plant. Never let it sit in wet soil.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 2–4 ft apart (2 ft for compact cultivars like 'Little Spire', 3–4 ft for full-size selections). Allow generous spacing for airflow — crowding in irrigated mixed borders is where problems start.[1]
care.bloom: Midsummer through fall on the Front Range — roughly July into October, with the lavender-blue haze at its peak in mid-to-late July. The silvery stems remain attractive well into winter. Deadheading is not required for repeat bloom; the plant blooms on new wood all season.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little to none. A light side-dressing of compost in early spring is the most that's needed. Avoid nitrogen-heavy fertilizers entirely — they promote lush, floppy growth and reduce the tight, silvery habit that makes this plant useful. Lean soil keeps it upright and free-blooming.[1]
care.maintenance: Leave the silver winter stems standing through the cold months — they protect the crown, mark where the plant sits, and look good with snow — then cut them down at the hard spring pruning.[1][2]
care.selfsow: May self-sow and also spreads slowly by rhizomes in favorable sites. Deadhead if you want to limit self-sowing, or choose a named compact cultivar ('Little Spire', 'Blue Jean Baby') which tend to be less vigorous spreaders. Unwanted seedlings are easy to hoe out in spring.[1][2]
care.troubles: Remarkably trouble-free — deer and rabbits avoid the strongly aromatic foliage, and it has virtually no serious pest or disease problems on the Front Range. Root rot from waterlogged soil is the one genuine risk (fix drainage). Occasional leafhoppers or aphids; nothing warranting treatment.[1][2]
care.companions: Classic Front Range combinations: Russian sage with ornamental grasses (little bluestem, blue grama), coneflower, catmint, globe thistle, sedum 'Autumn Joy', and lavender. It harmonizes with anything silver, blue, or yellow. For a xeric border: pair with rabbitbrush, penstemon, desert marigold, or cushion spurge.[1]
care.planting: Plant container-grown specimens in spring (April to mid-May) or in early fall (September) on the Front Range — both windows allow roots to establish before the stresses of summer heat or hard frost. Spring is the safer bet for gardeners new to this plant, as it gives the full growing season for the woody base to develop. In containers, Russian sage can survive a Front Range winter if the pot is large (roots need insulation), drainage is sharp, and the pot is sited against a south-facing wall or sheltered from desiccating winds. Small pots are not reliably hardy outdoors in winter — roots freeze solid in thin plastic containers. Water containers more frequently in summer than in-ground plants; dry out significantly in winter but don't let the woody crown desiccate completely.[1][2]
care.propagation: Softwood stem cuttings taken in late spring to early summer — when new growth is 4–6 in. long and still pliable — are the most reliable vegetative method. Take 3–5 in. nodal cuttings, strip the lower leaves, and root in a gritty, well-drained medium; bottom heat speeds things up. The plant can also be grown from seed, though named cultivars will not come true; sow fresh seed in fall or cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing. Division of the woody crown is generally not recommended — the base becomes densely woody with age and divisions rarely transplant successfully; stem cuttings are the preferred vegetative route. For naturalizing or filling a large space, allow existing plants to self-sow lightly and transplant volunteers before the taproot deepens.[1][2]
care.pruning: Hard spring pruning is the essential annual task, because Russian sage blooms on new wood. Wait until you see the first flush of new silver-green growth at the base — usually late March to mid-April around Boulder — then cut all of the previous year's stems back to 6–12 in above the ground (or to just above the emerging shoots). Unlike the lavenders and true sages, it resprouts readily from the old wood, so cutting into bare wood below the growth is fine; cutting too early risks removing emerging shoots. Do not skip the spring cut: left unpruned, the plant becomes an increasingly floppy, bare-based, sparse-blooming woody tangle. A light shaping after the first bloom flush is optional.[1][2]

edible.summary: Russian sage is an aromatic ornamental, not the culinary sage — it is not a recognized food plant and is best treated as inedible.[2]
edible.caution: Despite its common name and sage-like scent, Russian sage (*Salvia yangii*, formerly *Perovskia atriplicifolia*) is NOT culinary sage (*Salvia officinalis*) and is not a documented food plant. Its only recorded uses are ornamental and traditional-medicinal (in parts of Asia it has been used to treat fevers and other ailments), not culinary. The strongly aromatic foliage is rich in camphor and other volatile terpenes and can irritate skin on handling and cause gastrointestinal upset if eaten in quantity. Treat all parts as inedible; do not substitute it for cooking sage, and keep children from nibbling the leaves or flowers.[2]
